g.data-narration: Welcome to Combinational Logic. We explore circuits whose outputs depend solely on current inputs, forming the foundation of digital systems.
g.text: Building blocks for arithmetic operations, data processing & code conversion

g.data-narration: A combinational circuit's output depends only on present inputs, with no memory or feedback loops. Each input combination yields a unique output.
g.text: A combinational circuit has n inputs and m outputs. For every input combination at time t,
there is a unique output at time t. The circuit has no internal state or memory.
Output = f(Input₁, Input₂, ..., Inputₙ) — determined solely by current inputs.

g.data-narration: The block diagram shows n inputs entering the combinational circuit, which processes them through Boolean functions to produce m outputs.
The systematic design of combinational circuits follows five key steps: problem definition, I/O identification, truth table, Boolean functions, and logic diagram.
Step 1 defines what the circuit must accomplish. Step 2 identifies all input and output variables needed to solve the problem.
g.text: Clearly define what the circuit should do.
Identify number of input variables (n) and

g.data-narration: Step 3 creates a truth table with all 2^n input combinations. Step 4 derives simplified Boolean expressions from the truth table.
g.text: for each output from the truth table.
Use Boolean algebra or Karnaugh maps.

g.data-narration: Step 5 draws the final logic diagram using gates that implement the simplified Boolean functions from the previous step.
g.text: Draw the circuit implementing the simplified Boolean functions using logic gates.
AND, OR, NOT, NAND, NOR, XOR gates connect to realize the design.

g.data-narration: The Half Adder adds two 1-bit numbers. Sum uses XOR, Carry uses AND. This is the basic building block for binary addition.
The Full Adder adds three bits including Carry-in. Sum is XOR of all three, Carry-out is majority function of A, B, and Cin.
g.text: Two Half Adders + One OR Gate

g.data-narration: A Multiplexer selects one of many inputs and directs it to output. A 2^n-to-1 MUX has 2^n data inputs, n select lines, and 1 output.
g.text: A digital switch that selects ONE input from multiple sources and routes it to a single output.

g.data-narration: Decoders convert binary to one-hot output; encoders do reverse. Essential for memory addressing and instruction decoding in processors.
g.text: n inputs → 2ⁿ outputs. Only ONE output is HIGH for each input code.
2ⁿ inputs → n outputs. Generates binary code corresponding to active input.

g.data-narration: Let's design a 2-to-1 MUX: inputs I0, I1, select S. Output Y equals I0 when S=0, and I1 when S=1. Y = (S'·I0) + (S·I1).
Key takeaways: Combinational circuits have output determined only by current inputs with no memory. Design follows five systematic steps.
g.text: Definition: Output depends SOLELY on present inputs (no memory)
Design Flow: Problem → I/O → Truth Table → Boolean Simplification → Logic Diagram

g.data-narration: Essential circuits: MUX selects data, Decoder converts binary to one-hot, Encoder does reverse. These form ALU, control unit, and data path of processors.
g.text: Multiplexer: Data selector - 2ⁿ inputs, n selects → 1 output (Y=S'I₀+SI₁)
Decoder: n inputs → 2ⁿ outputs (one-hot). Used in memory addressing
Encoder: 2ⁿ inputs → n outputs. Inverse of decoder
Applications: ALU, control unit, data routing, address selection
